worth_it_explanation: Naming hygiene decides whether a publisher can slice reporting by placement, deal, or package without manual cross-referencing. Tag and placement names that follow no parseable pattern, or deal and package names that no one can identify on sight, turn every reporting question into a lookup exercise and make it easy to point demand at the wrong inventory. The check reviews names a publisher already has in OpenX, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a concrete rename or convention the publisher can adopt.
intro: Naming conventions are the labels a publisher attaches to ad tags, placements, deals, and packages so that reporting, troubleshooting, and demand targeting can find the right inventory without guesswork. OpenX runs Prebid-compatible header bidding, so the ad unit code a publisher chooses flows from the page into reporting and deal targeting, and a name that carries no structure cannot be parsed back into a placement, a page type, or a size. This subcategory covers whether tag and placement names follow a consistent, parseable pattern and whether deal and package names are identifiable, rather than the targeting or pricing those objects carry.

audit_checks.title: Ad tag and placement names follow no consistent, parseable convention
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Tag and placement names that carry no structure cannot be sliced in reporting without a manual lookup, so a publisher cannot read revenue or fill by page type, position, or size at a glance. The cost is operational rather than direct revenue loss, but it slows every diagnosis and raises the chance of pointing demand at the wrong placement.

educate.base:
An ad tag or placement name is the label that follows an impression through the auction and into reporting. In a Prebid-compatible setup like OpenX, the ad unit code a publisher assigns is what links the slot on the page to the auction and to the line that shows up in reports later. When that name carries structure, a reader can look at it and know the page type, the position, and the size without opening anything else. When it carries no structure, the name is just a string, and every question about it becomes a lookup.

A parseable convention encodes the facts that matter into a predictable order, separated by a consistent delimiter. A name might run from broad to narrow, for example the site or section, then the page type, then the position, then the size, so that 'homepage_leaderboard_728x90' reads the same way every time and a report can be filtered or grouped on any segment of it. The opposite is a set of names that each follow their own logic, where one slot is 'top_ad', another is 'div-gpt-ad-12345', and a third is 'newbanner2', so nothing can be grouped and the only way to know what a name means is to ask the person who created it.

Prebid's own guidance treats the ad unit code as a mixed attribute that can be either the ad slot name or the page div ID, and it introduced the Prebid Ad Slot and Global Placement ID (GPID) conventions precisely because an unstructured code is hard to use for reporting and auction targeting. A stable, descriptive name per slot is what makes fine-grained reporting and accurate deal targeting possible.

A few signs point to names that do not follow a parseable convention:
  • Tag and placement names mix several styles, so some encode position and size while others are generic strings or raw div IDs
  • Reporting cannot be grouped by page type, position, or size from the name alone and needs a separate spreadsheet to decode each one
  • No documented convention exists that a new placement is expected to follow, so each new name is created ad hoc
You may want to confirm that tag and placement names follow one documented, parseable convention. One approach is to define the order of segments and the delimiter once, then rename placements to match and apply the convention to every new slot going forward.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, inventory spans many sections and templates, so a section-front leaderboard, an in-article unit, and a gallery slot each behave differently in the auction. You may want a convention that encodes the section and page type into the name, so reporting can separate section-front performance from in-article performance without a manual mapping.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages reach a higher-intent audience than editorial pages and often clear at different prices. You may want the placement name to encode the page type, for example product, category, or editorial, so the higher-intent inventory can be read and targeted separately rather than blending into one undifferentiated report.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid Ad Slot and GPID for Header Bidding
Ad Unit Reference for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Review the ad tag and placement names across the inventory to see whether they follow one consistent, parseable pattern or several ad hoc styles.

openx_ui.label: OpenX publisher UI
steps: Open the inventory or ad tag section in the OpenX publisher console and list the active placements with their names.
Read the names side by side and note whether they share a consistent order of segments, such as section then page type then position then size, and a consistent delimiter.
Record any name that breaks the pattern, for example a generic label, a raw div ID, or a one-off string, and note which placement it belongs to.
Ask the publisher whether a documented naming convention exists that new placements are expected to follow, and record what it specifies.

page_source.label: Page source and Prebid config
steps: Open the page source or the Prebid configuration and read the ad unit code assigned to each slot.
Compare the ad unit codes against the names in the OpenX console to confirm they line up and carry the same structure.
Note any slot whose code is a raw div ID (e.g., 'div-gpt-ad-...') rather than a descriptive, parseable name. That code is what reporting and deal targeting will key on.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, across the active placements, whether the names follow one documented convention with a consistent segment order and delimiter, or whether they mix several styles. A set of names that all parse the same way and map to a documented convention passes. A set that mixes structured names with generic strings or raw div IDs, with no documented convention to follow, is a fail recorded with the placements that break the pattern.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the tag and placement names follow one documented, parseable convention and list the placements that break the pattern above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the ad tag and placement names on {{site_name}} do not follow one consistent, parseable convention, so reporting cannot be grouped by page type, position, or size from the name alone. In a Prebid-compatible setup, the ad unit code follows an impression into reporting and deal targeting, and a name with no structure has to be decoded by hand each time. {{voice_recommend}} defining one convention that encodes the segments that matter, for example section, page type, position, and size, in a fixed order with a consistent delimiter, then renaming the placements to match and applying the convention to every new slot. After the change, confirm reporting can be filtered and grouped on the name without a separate lookup.

audit_checks.title: Deal and package names are not consistent or identifiable
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Deal and package names that no one can identify on sight make it easy to point a buyer at the wrong inventory, troubleshoot the wrong deal, or let a stale package keep serving. The cost reaches revenue when a misidentified deal sends demand to the wrong placement or when a deal that should have ended cannot be found to pause it.

educate.base:
A deal or package name is how a publisher and a buyer both refer to the same agreement. A deal carries a buyer, an inventory scope, a price, and often a date range, and the name is the handle everyone uses to find it in reporting, in troubleshooting, and in conversations with the demand partner. When the name identifies those facts, anyone can pick the right deal out of a list. When it does not, the only way to know what a deal covers is to open it and read its targeting, which is slow and error-prone at the moment it matters most.

The risk shows up in two ways. The first is misdirection, where a buyer or an internal operator points demand at a deal whose name suggested one thing but whose targeting covers another, so impressions clear against the wrong inventory or at the wrong price. The second is staleness, where a deal that should have ended keeps serving because no one could find it in a list of similarly vague names to pause it. Both come back to the same gap, which is that the name did not carry enough to identify the deal without opening it.

The wider programmatic standards reflect how real this problem is. The IAB Tech Lab Deals API was created in part because each system named its deals differently, with notations that had to be decoded to understand the terms, and it sets out to standardize how deal metadata is communicated between an SSP and a DSP so the meaning travels with the deal. A publisher does not need the full API to apply the same idea internally, which is a consistent naming pattern that encodes the buyer, the inventory, and a version or date so a deal is identifiable on sight.

A few signs point to deal and package names that are not identifiable:
  • Deal and package names are generic, for example 'Deal 1' or 'Q3 package', so the buyer and the inventory cannot be read from the name
  • Two deals with similar names cover different inventory, which invites a buyer or operator to target the wrong one
  • Ended or paused deals are hard to distinguish from active ones because the name carries no status or date
You may want to confirm that deal and package names identify the buyer, the inventory, and a version or date on sight. One approach is to adopt one naming pattern for deals and packages, rename the active ones to match, and retire or clearly mark the names of deals that are no longer running.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a publisher often runs several deals with the same buyer across different sections or seasons, so 'Buyer_Homepage_Q3' and 'Buyer_Sports_Q3' need to be distinct on sight. You may want the deal name to encode the section or inventory scope alongside the buyer, so two deals with the same partner are never confused for each other.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, seasonal and category-specific deals come and go, so a name that carries the category and a date range helps separate an active holiday deal from one that should have ended. You may want deal names to encode the inventory and a version or date, so a stale deal is easy to find and pause rather than left serving past its term.

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's detailed configuration documentation for deals and packages is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the deal and package names to see whether they identify the buyer, the inventory, and a version or date, or whether they are generic and hard to tell apart.

openx_ui.label: OpenX publisher UI
steps: Open the deals or packages section in the OpenX publisher console and list the active and recently ended deals with their names.
For each deal, read whether the name identifies the buyer, the inventory scope, and a version or date, and note any that are generic or ambiguous.
Record any pair of deals whose names are similar but whose targeting differs because those invite a buyer or operator to select the wrong one.
Check whether ended or paused deals are distinguishable from active ones by name alone, or whether each has to be opened to learn its status.

openx_reporting.label: OpenX reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by deal or package with revenue, impressions, and the inventory served.
Confirm that each deal in the report can be identified from its name without opening it, and flag any that cannot.
Note any deal still serving that the publisher believes should have ended, which points to a stale deal that a clearer name would have surfaced.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, across the active and recently ended deals and packages, whether the names identify the buyer, the inventory, and a version or date, and whether similar names are kept distinct. A set of deal and package names that are identifiable on sight and follow one consistent pattern passes. Generic or ambiguous names, similar names covering different inventory, or stale deals indistinguishable from active ones are a fail recorded with the deals affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the deal and package names identify the buyer, inventory, and date on sight and list any that are generic or ambiguous above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the deal and package names on {{site_name}} are not consistent or identifiable, so a buyer or operator cannot tell which inventory a deal covers without opening it. That gap makes it easy to point demand at the wrong deal or to leave a stale deal serving because it cannot be found to pause it. {{voice_recommend}} adopting one naming pattern that encodes the buyer, the inventory scope, and a version or date, then renaming the active deals to match and clearly marking the names of deals that are no longer running. After the change, confirm each deal can be identified from its name in reporting without opening it.
